narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings—dreaming of law school only to have your application turned away feels like a door slamming shut just as you reach for the handle. Our prediction model looked at your profile and estimated a 65.9% chance you'd struggle to pass the bar exam, painting a picture of a path with more hurdles than we'd like for someone stepping into this demanding field. What stood out most was your Year 3 decile of 2.0, the strongest signal pulling toward this outcome, suggesting that by the end of your undergrad journey, you were ranking near the bottom in your school and hinting at challenges sustaining top performance under pressure. Right behind that was your Year 1 decile of 3.0, reinforcing a pattern of early struggles that didn't shift much over time. Your LSAT score of 34 added to the concern, as it fell short of the benchmarks that typically light the way to bar success for others. Even with your upper-middle family income, which might offer some support, it didn't outweigh these academic flags in the model's eyes—in fact, it nudged slightly toward risk too. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.6 layered on another worry, showing a foundation that the model saw as shaky for the rigors ahead. Being a white male pursuing full-time studies aligned with patterns we've seen struggle more often than not in this prediction space. It's not that any one thing doomed your chances, but this combination told a story of inconsistent academic momentum that echoed too many past journeys ending short of the bar. The good news? You're not defined by a number—you've got time to rebuild that story, maybe by targeting those deciles with focused prep or retaking the LSAT. Law school isn't the only door; keep pushing, because your determination can rewrite what comes next. We're rooting for you.
